note: 19th century; sounds as trivial as it is

d: Nonsense; talk or writing that is pointless
note: Old English; originally meant to drool

d: Skillful flattery; charming talk intended to deceive
note: From the Blarney Stone in County Cork, Ireland

d: A person who sells quack medicines; a charlatan
note: Italian montambanco meaning mount on a bench to sell

d: An utter coward
note: French poltron from Italian poltrone meaning sluggard

d: A person who is timid and lacks courage or decisiveness
note: Medieval; a sop soaked in milk equals something too soft

d: A foolish or simple-minded person
note: 16th century; possibly clipped from innocent

d: A stupid person
note: From numb plus skull; 18th century

d: A stupid or slow-witted person
note: From the supposedly dim nature of sheep

d: A person who tends to be disorganized and forgetful
note: Self-evident compound; 18th century

d: Rash or impractical; flighty and poorly thought out
note: From the supposed flightiness of hares; 16th century

d: To act or proceed with excessive caution
note: From a cat's careful, quiet, deliberate tread

d: To be unable to make up one's mind
note: From shall I, shall I; reduplication of indecision

d: A small device or object whose name is unknown
note: Afrikaans ding meaning thing via American slang

d: A gadget or small mechanical device
note: 1940s American slang; origin entirely unknown

d: A person who boasts loudly and at great length
note: American; the blowing part is almost literal

d: A naive, unsophisticated person from the countryside
note: American slang clipped from the name Reuben

d: Nonsense; a confidence trick or swindle
note: Reduplication in use since the 16th century

d: A stupid or mediocre boxer; a large clumsy person
note: American boxing slang; origin debated

d: A mischievous or disreputable person
note: Variant of scalawag; Civil War era American

d: An unprincipled rogue or scoundrel
note: Old French valet gone bad; the disreputable servant

d: A person who behaves badly but in an endearing way
note: 18th century; from to scamp meaning to do work carelessly

d: Unwilling to speak directly or honestly; evasive
note: From having meal, a soft grain, in the mouth
